title: Fractions
summary: Fractions are one of the most useful ideas in everyday math because they describe parts of a whole, comparisons between quantities, and numbers that are not written as whole numbers. A fraction like 1/2 means one equal part out of two, but fractions can also represent division, ratios, and measurements. People use them in cooking, shopping, construction, and many kinds of problem solving. The topic becomes easier once you understand how the numerator and denominator work and why equal parts matter. Learning fractions well also helps with decimals, percentages, and algebra later on.

concepts.title: What Fractions Are
concepts.summary: A fraction represents a part of a whole. The denominator tells how many equal parts the whole has been divided into; the numerator tells how many of those parts you are counting. The equal-parts requirement is not a detail — it is the foundation. Without it, the denominator has no fixed meaning and the fraction communicates nothing. Once that principle is clear, fractions become readable as single quantities rather than as two unrelated numbers.

sections.title: What it is
body: A fraction is a number that represents a part of a whole. When you cut a pizza into eight equal slices and take three of them, you have taken 3/8 of the pizza. The bottom number — the denominator — tells you how many equal parts the whole has been divided into. The top number — the numerator — tells you how many of those parts you are counting. Together, the two numbers express a single quantity: a specific portion of something.

Fractions appear whenever a whole cannot do the job alone — whenever you need to describe something between zero and one, or between any two whole numbers. Half a cup of flour. Three-quarters of an hour. Two and a half miles. Seven out of ten students. In every case, the fraction communicates a relationship: how many pieces you have out of how many pieces exist.

sections.title: The rule
body: One rule governs everything about fractions: the parts must be equal. A fraction is only meaningful when the whole has been divided into pieces of identical size. Equal division is not a detail — it is the entire foundation. Without it, the denominator has no fixed meaning.

A second rule follows from this: the denominator sets the unit size. The larger the denominator, the smaller each piece. One-half (1/2) means the whole is split into two pieces; one-eighth (1/8) means it is split into eight. This means that a larger denominator produces a smaller fraction when the numerator stays the same — 1/8 is less than 1/2 — because more divisions mean smaller pieces.

sections.title: Worked example
body: Imagine a chocolate bar divided into 12 equal squares. You eat 4 squares. You have eaten 4/12 of the bar. The denominator (12) tells you the bar is divided into 12 equal pieces. The numerator (4) tells you how many of those pieces you took. Together: 4 out of 12 equal parts.

Now compare 4/12 to 1/3. These look different, but they represent the same amount. If you group the 12 squares into sets of four, you get three groups — so 4 squares is exactly one of three equal groups, which is 1/3. Two fractions that look different but name the same quantity are called equivalent fractions. 4/12 = 1/3. You can always create an equivalent fraction by multiplying or dividing both the numerator and denominator by the same number.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: The most common mistake is treating the numerator and denominator as independent numbers rather than as one relationship. Students see 3/4 and compare it to 3/5 by looking only at the denominators, concluding that 3/5 is bigger because 5 is bigger than 4. But a larger denominator means smaller pieces. 3/5 means three pieces out of five; 3/4 means three pieces out of four. Each piece in 3/4 is bigger, so 3/4 is the larger fraction.

A second common mistake is forgetting the equal-parts requirement when drawing or interpreting fraction models. If someone draws a circle divided into four unequal sections and shades two of them, the shaded portion is not necessarily 1/2 — it depends on the actual sizes. The number of sections is only meaningful when those sections are identical.

sections.title: Try it yourself
body: Draw a rectangle and divide it into six equal parts. Shade three of them. What fraction is shaded? Now shade four of six. Which fraction is larger — the first or the second? How do you know? Try the same exercise on a number line: mark 0 and 1, then mark the point that represents 3/4. Where does it fall relative to 1/2?

sections.title: Where it shows up
body: Fractions appear every time a whole must be divided into meaningful pieces: cooking measurements, sale discounts, time fractions, sports statistics, map scales, and probability. Every concept in this topic builds on this foundation. Comparing fractions requires understanding what makes one fraction bigger than another. Operating with fractions requires understanding what happens when you combine or divide quantities expressed as parts. Converting to decimals and percentages requires recognizing that all three notations describe the same underlying relationship.

concepts.title: Comparing Fractions
concepts.summary: Comparing fractions means deciding which is larger, smaller, or whether they are equal. Three strategies cover all cases: same denominator (compare numerators directly), same numerator (smaller denominator wins because pieces are larger), and common denominator (convert both fractions, then compare numerators). The benchmark fraction 1/2 provides a fast shortcut: if one fraction is above 1/2 and the other is below, the comparison is immediate.

sections.title: What it is
body: Comparing fractions means deciding which of two fractions is larger, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. This is not always obvious from looking at the numbers. 3/8 and 2/5 both look small, but deciding which is bigger requires more than a glance. Several strategies make comparison manageable, and the right strategy depends on the specific fractions you are working with.

The ability to compare fractions is important because math rarely presents numbers in isolation. You need to compare fractions when choosing between measurements, checking whether an answer is reasonable, ordering a list of values, or deciding whether a fraction is more or less than a benchmark like 1/2 or 1.

sections.title: The rule
body: Three comparison strategies cover all situations. Strategy one — same denominator: if two fractions share a denominator, the larger numerator wins. 5/8 > 3/8 because both fractions have pieces of the same size, so more pieces means more. Strategy two — same numerator: if two fractions share a numerator, the smaller denominator wins. 3/4 > 3/8 because the numerators are both 3, but fourths are larger pieces than eighths — three larger pieces beat three smaller pieces. Strategy three — common denominator: when neither the numerators nor the denominators match, rewrite both fractions so they share a denominator, then compare numerators. To compare 2/3 and 3/5, find a common multiple of 3 and 5 (15). Rewrite: 2/3 = 10/15 and 3/5 = 9/15. Now compare: 10/15 > 9/15, so 2/3 > 3/5.

A useful shortcut alongside these strategies is the benchmark 1/2. Ask whether each fraction is above or below one-half. If one is above and the other is below, the comparison is immediate without any conversion.

sections.title: Worked example
body: Compare 5/6 and 7/9. Neither denominators nor numerators match, and both are above 1/2, so the benchmark doesn't separate them. Find a common denominator: the least common multiple of 6 and 9 is 18. Rewrite: 5/6 = 15/18 and 7/9 = 14/18. Compare: 15/18 > 14/18. So 5/6 > 7/9.

A faster alternative: 5/6 is missing 1/6 from a whole, while 7/9 is missing 2/9. Is 1/6 or 2/9 the larger gap? 1/6 = 3/18 and 2/9 = 4/18. The gap for 7/9 is bigger, meaning 7/9 is further from 1, so 5/6 is closer to 1 and therefore larger. This distance-from-1 shortcut avoids full conversion when both fractions are close to 1.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: The most common mistake is comparing denominators directly without accounting for their effect on piece size. A student might conclude 3/7 > 3/5 because 7 > 5. In fact 3/5 > 3/7 because fifths are larger pieces than sevenths. Whenever the numerators are equal, the comparison runs opposite to what the denominators might suggest at first glance.

A second common mistake is forgetting to adjust the numerator when converting to a common denominator. To convert 2/3 to twelfths, multiply the denominator by 4 to get 12 — you must also multiply the numerator by 4 to get 8, giving 8/12. Students who multiply only the denominator change the fraction's value and reach wrong conclusions.

sections.title: Try it yourself
body: Without converting to common denominators, use the same-denominator or same-numerator strategy wherever possible: which is larger, 4/9 or 4/7? Which is larger, 5/8 or 7/8? Now use the benchmark: is 3/7 above or below 1/2? Is 5/9 above or below 1/2? Finally, use common denominators to compare 3/4 and 5/7. What is the least common multiple of 4 and 7? What do the fractions become after conversion?

sections.title: Where it shows up
body: Comparing fractions appears in cooking (which measuring cup holds more?), construction (which gap is narrower?), finance (which discount is larger?), and any time you need to rank or order fractional values. The skill is also a prerequisite for adding and subtracting fractions with unlike denominators — you need to understand why equal denominators are required before the procedure makes sense.

concepts.title: Adding, Subtracting, Multiplying, and Dividing Fractions
concepts.summary: Each fraction operation follows its own logic. Addition and subtraction require a common denominator because you can only combine pieces of the same size. Multiplication does not — multiply numerators together and denominators together, and the product is smaller than either original fraction when both are proper fractions. Division uses the reciprocal rule: flip the second fraction and multiply. Understanding why each rule works, not just what it is, is what makes the procedures memorable.

sections.title: What it is
body: Fractions can be added, subtracted, multiplied, and divided just as whole numbers can — but the procedures are different for each operation, and mixing them up is one of the most common sources of fraction errors. Each procedure has its own logic grounded in what the operation means, and understanding that logic is what makes the rules stick rather than seeming arbitrary.

sections.title: The rule
body: Addition and subtraction require a common denominator. You cannot add 1/3 and 1/4 directly because the pieces are different sizes — adding thirds and fourths is like adding centimeters and inches without converting. First convert both fractions to the same denominator, then add the numerators. The denominator stays the same because the piece size does not change when you combine pieces of the same size. To add 1/3 + 1/4: find a common denominator (12), convert (4/12 + 3/12), add the numerators (7/12). Subtraction works identically.

Multiplication does not require a common denominator: multiply numerators together and denominators together, then simplify. 2/3 × 3/5 = 6/15 = 2/5. You can also cancel common factors across numerators and denominators before multiplying to keep the numbers smaller. Division uses the reciprocal rule: flip the second fraction and multiply. To divide 3/4 ÷ 2/5, rewrite as 3/4 × 5/2 = 15/8. For mixed numbers, convert to improper fractions first (2 3/4 = 11/4), then apply the appropriate operation.

sections.title: Worked example
body: Addition: 2/5 + 1/3. Common denominator of 5 and 3 is 15. Rewrite: 6/15 + 5/15 = 11/15. Subtraction: 3/4 − 2/7. Common denominator is 28. Rewrite: 21/28 − 8/28 = 13/28. Multiplication: 3/8 × 4/9. Cancel first: divide 3 and 9 by 3, divide 4 and 8 by 4. This leaves 1/2 × 1/3 = 1/6. Without canceling: 12/72 = 1/6 — same answer, more arithmetic.

Division: 5/6 ÷ 3/4. Flip the second fraction: 5/6 × 4/3 = 20/18 = 10/9 = 1 1/9. Notice that the product of two proper fractions (multiplication) is smaller than either original — 3/8 × 4/9 = 1/6, which is less than both 3/8 and 4/9. This is a useful reasonableness check: if you multiply two fractions less than 1 and your product is larger than either, something went wrong.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: The most common mistake in addition and subtraction is adding or subtracting the denominators as well as the numerators: 1/3 + 1/5 ≠ 2/8. The denominator represents piece size, which does not change when you combine pieces of the same size — only the count (numerator) changes. A quick reasonableness check catches this error: 2/8 = 1/4, which is less than 1/3 alone, so it cannot be the sum of 1/3 and 1/5.

The most common mistake in division is flipping the wrong fraction — flipping the first instead of the second, or forgetting to flip at all. The rule is always: flip the divisor (the second fraction — the one you are dividing by) and multiply. A second common mistake across all operations is not simplifying the final answer. 12/72 and 1/6 are equal, but 1/6 is far easier to work with in any subsequent step.

sections.title: Try it yourself
body: Work each of these: 1/4 + 3/8 (find the common denominator first). 5/6 − 1/4. 3/5 × 2/3 (try canceling before you multiply). 7/8 ÷ 1/4 (what does dividing by 1/4 mean? How many quarter-pieces fit in seven-eighths?). After each, check whether the answer is reasonable: is your sum larger than either original fraction? Is your product smaller than both originals when both are proper fractions?

sections.title: Where it shows up
body: Fraction operations appear constantly in cooking (halving a recipe means dividing all measurements by 2), construction (adding lengths expressed in fractions of inches), probability (combining probabilities, which are fractions), and algebra (working with algebraic fractions in equations). The multiplication rule — that the product of two proper fractions is smaller than either — is the foundation for understanding probability: the chance of two independent events both occurring is always less than the chance of either alone.

concepts.title: Fractions, Decimals, and Percentages
concepts.summary: A fraction, a decimal, and a percentage are three different notations for the same kind of quantity. Converting between them requires four procedures: fraction to decimal (divide numerator by denominator), decimal to percentage (multiply by 100), percentage to decimal (divide by 100), and decimal to fraction (read the place value, write as a fraction, simplify). A small set of benchmark conversions — 1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/3, 1/5, 1/8, 1/10 — appears so frequently that they are worth memorizing directly.

sections.title: What it is
body: A fraction, a decimal, and a percentage can all represent the same quantity. One-half, 0.5, and 50% are three different ways to write the same number. They are not different things — they are different notations for the same idea: a part of a whole. Being able to move fluently between all three forms is one of the most practical math skills, because different contexts favor different forms. Recipes use fractions. Prices use decimals. Test scores and statistics use percentages. Being stuck in one notation limits your ability to work across all of them.

sections.title: The rule
body: Fraction to decimal: divide the numerator by the denominator. 3/4 = 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75. 1/3 = 1 ÷ 3 = 0.333 (repeating). 5/8 = 5 ÷ 8 = 0.625. Decimal to percentage: multiply by 100 and add the % symbol. 0.75 → 75%. 0.333 → 33.3%. Percentage to decimal: divide by 100. 45% → 0.45. 7% → 0.07. 125% → 1.25. Decimal to fraction: read the place value, write as a fraction, simplify. 0.75 is seventy-five hundredths = 75/100 = 3/4. 0.6 is six tenths = 6/10 = 3/5. Percentage to fraction: write over 100 and simplify. 25% = 25/100 = 1/4. 60% = 60/100 = 3/5.

sections.title: Worked example
body: You are comparing two sale discounts: one item is marked '1/3 off,' another is marked '30% off.' Convert 1/3 to a percentage: 1 ÷ 3 = 0.333, times 100 = 33.3%. Compare: 33.3% vs. 30%. The first discount is slightly larger.

You are baking and a recipe calls for 0.625 cups of an ingredient. Your measuring cups are marked in fractions. 0.625 = 625/1000 = 5/8. Use the 5/8 cup measure. A student scores 17 out of 20 on a quiz: 17/20 = 17 ÷ 20 = 0.85 = 85%.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: The most common mistake is moving the decimal point in the wrong direction when converting between decimals and percentages. Percent means 'per hundred,' so going from decimal to percent multiplies by 100 (0.45 → 45%, not 0.0045). Going from percent to decimal divides by 100 (45% → 0.45, not 45). Keeping the direction straight requires remembering which conversion multiplies and which divides.

A second common mistake is misreading place value when converting decimals to fractions. 0.3 is three-tenths (3/10), not three-hundredths (3/100). 0.03 is three-hundredths. The number of decimal places tells you the denominator: one decimal place = tenths, two = hundredths, three = thousandths. A third mistake is failing to simplify the resulting fraction. 0.6 = 6/10 is valid but 3/5 is the simplified form and easier to work with.

sections.title: Try it yourself
body: Convert each to all three forms. Start with 3/8: fraction to decimal (divide), then decimal to percent (multiply by 100). Then 0.4: decimal to fraction (place value and simplify), then to percent. Then 65%: percent to decimal (divide by 100), then to fraction (over 100, simplify). Check your benchmarks from memory without calculating: what decimal is 1/4 equal to? What percentage is 3/4? What fraction is 20%?

sections.title: Where it shows up
body: This skill appears in virtually every real-world math context: calculating tips (percentages), reading nutrition labels (% daily value), interpreting poll results, measuring in construction (fractions of inches), calculating discounts, and understanding probability. The ability to switch fluidly between forms also makes algebra easier, because algebraic fractions, decimal coefficients, and percentage-based word problems are all versions of the same underlying quantity system.
